core_intuitions: The three learning paradigms differ only in where the supervision signal comes from; self-supervision manufactures labels from the input itself, which is what makes LLM pretraining possible.

Test error decomposes into bias² + variance + irreducible noise; as model complexity grows bias falls while variance rises, so the two trade off and their sum is a U-shaped curve minimized at intermediate complexity.

Underfitting is high bias (both train and test loss high, model too simple); overfitting is high variance (train loss low, test loss high, model memorized noise) — the gap between the two curves is the generalization gap.

L2 and L1 penalties differ geometrically: the L2 ball is round so the solution shrinks smoothly toward the origin, while the L1 diamond has sharp corners on the axes so the solution snaps to exactly-zero coordinates, producing sparsity.
A confusion matrix's four cells (TP/FP/FN/TN) are the atoms from which precision, recall, F1, TPR and FPR are all built; which errors you care about determines which metric you optimize.

The ROC curve is what you get by sweeping the decision threshold from 1 to 0 and plotting TPR vs FPR at each setting; AUC is the area under it and equals the probability a random positive is ranked above a random negative.

existing.name: mlfund-paradigm-spectrum
existing.issue: In the dark render, the two annotation phrases ('Supervision signal availability' and 'sits at the intersection') sit close to the dashed connector lines and the faint gradient-bar dots, so the crossing is slightly busy — but everything remains legible and reads correctly in both themes.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only: nudge the two annotation labels a few px clear of the dashed connectors, or lighten the connectors, so the dark theme reads as cleanly as the light one. Not required.

existing.name: mlfund-confusion-matrix
existing.issue: Correct/error is encoded with semantic green/red fills, which works and reads in both themes; the colored cell-label text (green TP/TN, red FP/FN) is a touch low-contrast on the dark fills but still legible. The 2x2 with hit/miss/false-alarm/correct-reject glosses is exactly the right intuition and directly seeds the precision/recall/ROC discussion that follows.
existing.improvement: Optional: bump the in-cell label text toward the theme foreground (keep the border/fill carrying the green/red semantics) so contrast is uniform across themes. Not required to keep.

new_figures.name: mlfund-bias-variance-decomposition
new_figures.concept: The bias-variance tradeoff shown two ways at once: how model complexity shapes the fitted function, and how bias² and variance sum to a U-shaped test-error curve.
new_figures.rationale: This is the analytical centerpiece of the chapter — an entire section, a decomposition equation, code, and the double-descent note — yet it has NO figure. The whole idea is inherently visual (a wiggly overfit curve vs a rigid underfit line) and the U-shaped sum is the single most cited picture in classical ML. A reader who sees this once internalizes the tradeoff far faster than from the equation alone.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The Bias-Variance Tradeoff', immediately after the sentence 'The optimal model complexity minimizes their sum.' (before the code block)
new_figures.brief: A two-panel figure sharing one visual story. TOP ROW: three small side-by-side scatter-plot panels over the same data. Draw ~10 sample points (small dots) scattered around a smooth ground-truth curve (a gentle sine-like wave, drawn as a faint dashed reference line labelled 'true function f*'). Panel 1 titled 'Underfit · high bias': overlay a single straight line that clearly misses the wave's shape. Panel 2 titled 'Good fit · balanced': overlay a smooth curve that tracks the wave well. Panel 3 titled 'Overfit · high variance': overlay a wildly wiggly curve that threads through every point including the noise. In panel 3 optionally draw two or three faint additional wiggly curves (different training draws) fanning apart to make 'sensitive to the training set' visible — they should visibly disagree with each other, whereas in panel 2 a couple of faint alternate curves would nearly overlap. BOTTOM PANEL: a single axes, x-axis labelled 'model complexity' (with tick hints 'simple' at left, 'complex' at right), y-axis labelled 'error'. Plot three curves: a 'bias²' curve that starts high on the left and decays toward zero on the right; a 'variance' curve that starts near zero on the left and rises on the right; and a 'total test error = bias² + variance (+ noise)' curve that is their sum — a clear U shape. Draw a faint horizontal dashed line near the bottom labelled 'irreducible noise σ²' as the floor the total curve cannot go below. Mark the minimum of the U with a vertical guide line down to the axis labelled 'sweet spot' / 'optimal complexity', and align it visually under the 'Good fit' top panel (i.e., left third of the x-axis ≈ underfit, middle ≈ good, right third ≈ overfit) so the two panels tell one continuous story. Use only theme-safe foreground/accent tokens; distinguish the three bottom curves by line style (solid / dashed / dotted) plus small inline labels rather than relying on hardcoded colors. No invented numeric axis values — axes are qualitative (only the words simple→complex, low→high). Everything must fit the viewBox with clear reading order top→bottom.

new_figures.name: mlfund-l1-l2-geometry
new_figures.concept: Why L1 regularization produces sparsity and L2 does not, shown as constraint-region geometry: loss contours meeting a round L2 ball vs a sharp-cornered L1 diamond.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter states L1 'promotes sparsity: many parameters are driven to exactly zero' and L2 'shrinks toward zero' but gives only the formulas and a Bayesian aside. The geometric picture is THE canonical intuition for why the two behave differently, and it cannot be gotten from the algebra. One glance at the diamond's corner sitting on an axis explains automatic feature selection instantly.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Regularization', at the end of the 'L1 Regularization (Lasso)' subsection (after the sentence ending 'standard for linear models and feature selection.')
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side panels sharing identical axes: a 2D weight space with horizontal axis labelled 'w₁' and vertical axis labelled 'w₂', origin at center with light crosshair axes. In BOTH panels draw the same set of concentric elliptical loss contours (3–4 nested tilted ellipses) centered on a point OFF the origin (up and to the right), with the center marked and labelled 'unregularized optimum ŵ' (the least-squares solution). LEFT panel titled 'L2 (ridge): round ball → shrinks, stays dense': draw a circle centered at the origin representing the constraint region ‖w‖₂ ≤ t (light fill). Mark the point where the outermost reachable loss contour is tangent to the circle — this tangency point is the L2 solution; it lies OFF both axes (both w₁ and w₂ nonzero but shrunken toward origin). Draw a small dot and a short callout 'both weights small, neither exactly 0'. RIGHT panel titled 'L1 (lasso): diamond corners → exact zeros (sparsity)': draw a diamond (rotated square, the ‖w‖₁ ≤ t ball) centered at the origin with its four vertices ON the axes. Show the loss contour touching the diamond at its TOP vertex, which sits exactly on the w₂ axis — so the solution has w₁ = 0. Mark that vertex with a dot and a callout 'w₁ = 0 exactly → feature dropped'. The teaching contrast is entirely in the shape: round region → generic tangency off-axis; pointed region → tangency at a corner on an axis. Keep contours identical across panels so the only difference the eye sees is ball vs diamond. Use theme-safe tokens only: the constraint region as a translucent accent fill, contours as thin foreground strokes, no hardcoded reds/greens/blues. Small illustrative geometry only, no numbers on the axes. Ensure the callout labels do not overlap the contours; place them outside the constraint region with short leader lines.

new_figures.name: mlfund-roc-threshold-sweep
new_figures.concept: The ROC curve is traced by sweeping the decision threshold from 1 to 0; each threshold yields one (FPR, TPR) point, and AUC is the resulting area.
new_figures.rationale: ROC/AUC is described purely in prose in the chapter. The defining intuition — that ROC is not one operating point but a whole family swept out by moving the threshold — is exactly the kind of process a static frame conveys weakly and motion conveys naturally: as the threshold slides, the two overlapping score distributions get cut at a moving line, the confusion-matrix counts change, and the dot walks up the ROC curve. This turns 'AUC is a threshold-free summary' from words into a felt mechanism, and reinforces the confusion-matrix figure that precedes it.
new_figures.placement: Section 'ROC and AUC', after the paragraph ending 'ranks a randomly chosen positive example higher than a randomly chosen negative.'
new_figures.brief: Two coupled panels animated together, driven by one moving threshold. LEFT panel: two overlapping, bell-shaped score distributions on a horizontal axis labelled 'model score ŵ(x) →', one hump labelled 'negatives' centered left, one hump labelled 'positives' centered right, with a realistic overlap region in the middle. A vertical 'threshold' line sits on this axis; everything to its RIGHT is predicted positive, to the LEFT predicted negative. Shade/annotate the four regions relative to the line so TP (positives right of line), FP (negatives right of line), FN (positives left), TN (negatives left) are readable — tie this back to the confusion-matrix figure's vocabulary. RIGHT panel: ROC axes, x-axis 'False Positive Rate (FPR)' 0→1, y-axis 'True Positive Rate (TPR)' 0→1, with the diagonal chance line dashed and labelled 'random (AUC 0.5)', and the ideal corner (0,1) marked. ANIMATION: sweep the threshold line in the left panel from far right (everything predicted negative) to far left (everything predicted positive). As it moves: (a) the shaded TP/FP/FN/TN regions update, and (b) a dot in the right panel walks along the ROC curve, tracing it out from (0,0) up to (1,1), with the area under the traced portion progressively filling to visualize AUC. Show the ROC curve accumulating (a trail) so by the end the full bowed curve above the diagonal is drawn and the filled area is labelled 'AUC'. Include two brief labelled pauses: one at a high threshold ('strict: few positives → low FPR, low TPR, bottom-left') and one at a low threshold ('lenient: many positives → high TPR but high FPR, top-right'). CRITICAL static-readability requirement: the final frame must stand alone — full ROC curve drawn, AUC area filled and labelled, threshold line at a representative middle position with the four regions labelled, chance diagonal shown. Use only theme-safe tokens; distinguish the two distributions and the four regions by fill opacity + labels, not hardcoded colors; keep the AUC fill a translucent accent. No invented AUC numbers beyond the qualitative 0.5 chance reference. Keep both panels inside the viewBox with the left (mechanism) reading before the right (result).

summary: The chapter is broad and concept-rich but figure-poor: two solid existing figures (paradigm spectrum, confusion matrix) both KEEP and read correctly in light and dark. The two most foundational ideas — the bias-variance decomposition (an entire centerpiece section with an equation and code but no visual) and the geometry of why L1 produces sparsity while L2 does not — have no figures and are exactly the ideas that a single well-made static infographic makes 'click.' Those are the two priority-1 additions. A third figure animates the ROC curve as a threshold sweep, the one concept here where motion (the moving threshold cutting the score distributions while the dot walks up the curve) genuinely conveys the mechanism better than a static frame, while still standing alone as a final frame. No changes to existing figures required beyond optional contrast polish.
